problem_statement: GET /flights/search is returning flights with non-scheduled statuses (cancelled/delayed) and excluding scheduled flights, so customers cannot see bookable inventory.
goal: Make flight search return only flights whose status is SCHEDULED.
acceptance_criteria: Calling GET /flights/search returns only flights with SCHEDULED status.
Cancelled, delayed, and other non-scheduled flights are excluded from search results.
Search results include scheduled flights when the database contains a mix of flight statuses.
constraints: Fix should be localized to the flight search implementation path with minimal surface area.
Affected file is indicated as backend/app/services/flight_service.py, specifically search_flights.
unknowns: Exact status field name and enum values in the database/model layer.
Whether filtering is applied in SQL/query construction or post-query transformation.
Whether there are companion tests already covering flight search status filtering.
repo_summary: Monorepo with backend service code under backend/app, including API routes, models, schemas, services, seed scripts, migrations, and tests. The issue points to backend/app/services/flight_service.py as the likely implementation target.

targeted_symbols.reason: This is the explicitly affected implementation area and the search logic likely needs a status filter correction to include only SCHEDULED flights.
Needed to confirm the status field name/type and valid status values used by search filtering.
May define persisted status column and help verify whether filtering should occur at query level.
Likely location to add/adjust regression coverage for scheduled-only search results.

localization_rationale: The issue description directly names backend/app/services/flight_service.py (search_flights) as the affected file.
The observed behavior is a classic search/filter bug, so the smallest likely fix is in the search query or result filtering logic rather than routes, schemas, or migrations.
Model/schema files are secondary candidates only to verify the status field and write a regression test.
The API route file is less likely to contain the bug because the endpoint behavior suggests incorrect service-layer filtering, not request handling.
